name: Romania
tagline: From Roman Dacia to the European Union — a Latin island in a Slavic and Magyar sea, unified, dismembered and remade.
summary: Romania's history is the story of a Latin-speaking people forged on the lower Danube and repeatedly contested by its larger neighbours. Its origins reach back to the kingdom of the Dacians, conquered by the Roman emperor Trajan in 101–106 CE, whose colonists left a Romance language and a sense of 'Romanness' that survived the empire's withdrawal. In the fourteenth century two Romanian principalities, Wallachia and Moldavia, emerged south and east of the Carpathians, producing rulers such as Vlad III the Impaler and Stephen the Great before falling under centuries of Ottoman suzerainty and the venal Phanariot regime. The two principalities chose the same prince, Alexandru Ioan Cuza, in 1859, forming the modern state; full independence was recognised at the Congress of Berlin in 1878 and a kingdom proclaimed in 1881. The First World War brought 'Greater Romania', crowned by the union of Transylvania on 1 December 1918 and confirmed by the Treaty of Trianon in 1920. In 1940 the country was torn apart — Bessarabia and northern Bukovina seized by the USSR, northern Transylvania awarded to Hungary, southern Dobruja ceded to Bulgaria. After four decades of communism and the dictatorship of Nicolae Ceausescu, a violent revolution in 1989 toppled the regime, and a democratic Romania joined NATO in 2004 and the European Union in 2007.

founding.label: The Union of the Principalities under Alexandru Ioan Cuza
founding.detail: Modern Romania was born from the union of the two Danubian principalities, Moldavia and Wallachia. On 5 January 1859 the elective assembly of Moldavia chose Alexandru Ioan Cuza as its prince, and on 24 January 1859 the assembly of Wallachia elected the very same man, creating a personal union that bound the two states into a single nation. Cuza set in motion a sweeping programme of modernisation — reform of the justice and fiscal systems, an agrarian reform that gave land to some 400,000 peasant families, the metric system and a French-modelled civil code — and the United Principalities increasingly used the name Romania. Though Cuza was forced to abdicate in 1866, the state he founded endured: its full independence was recognised by the Congress of Berlin in 1878, and it was proclaimed a kingdom in 1881.

quickFacts.label: Ancient origins
quickFacts.value: Roman province of Dacia, conquered by Trajan in 101–106 CE, source of the Romanian language

quickFacts.label: Medieval principalities
quickFacts.value: Wallachia (Basarab I, 14th c.) and Moldavia (Bogdan I, c. 1359)

quickFacts.label: Birth of the modern state
quickFacts.value: Union of Moldavia and Wallachia under Alexandru Ioan Cuza, 1859

quickFacts.label: Independence recognised
quickFacts.value: Congress of Berlin, 1878; Kingdom of Romania proclaimed 1881

quickFacts.label: Great Union
quickFacts.value: Union of Transylvania with Romania, 1 December 1918, confirmed by the Treaty of Trianon, 1920

quickFacts.label: Euro-Atlantic integration
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO on 29 March 2004 and the European Union on 1 January 2007

eras.title: Dacia and Rome: the Latin foundation
eras.standfirst: A powerful Dacian kingdom in the Carpathian arc was conquered by Trajan, and Roman colonists implanted the Latin tongue that would become Romanian.
body: Before Rome, the lands of the Carpathians and the lower Danube were held by the Dacians, a Thracian people who under King Burebista and later King Decebalus built a formidable kingdom centred on a cluster of mountain fortresses in the Orastie Mountains, its capital at Sarmizegetusa Regia. Rich in gold and silver and dangerously close to the Roman frontier, the Dacian kingdom drew the attention of the empire. The fortresses, an unusual fusion of military and religious architecture blending classical and late Iron Age techniques, formed the nucleus of the kingdom and are today inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List.

The emperor Trajan resolved to destroy Dacian power in two great wars. In the first campaign of 101–102 CE he forced Decebalus to terms and installed a Roman garrison in the capital; when the king broke the peace, Trajan returned, and by 106 CE the whole country was subdued and Decebalus took his own life to avoid capture. Trajan made Dacia a Roman province north of the Danube, commemorating the victory on the great spiral column that still stands in Rome and exploiting the province's mines and its enormous booty.

For roughly 170 years Dacia was Roman. Colonists drawn 'from all over the Roman world' settled the province, and Latin became the language of administration, law and daily life. Although the emperor Aurelian withdrew the Roman army and administration from Dacia between 271 and 275 CE under pressure on the frontiers, the Romanised population and its Latin speech persisted; that language is the direct ancestor of modern Romanian, the basis of the Daco-Roman account of the Romanian people's origins and of Romania's identity as a Latin nation in eastern Europe.

events.title: The Dacian kingdom
events.summary: The Dacians built a powerful kingdom in the Carpathian arc, fortified by stone strongholds in the Orastie Mountains with their capital at Sarmizegetusa Regia, the nucleus of Dacian power.

events.title: Trajan's first Dacian war
events.summary: The emperor Trajan invaded Dacia and forced King Decebalus to terms, installing a Roman garrison in the Dacian capital at Sarmizegetusa.

events.title: Conquest of Dacia and the Roman province
events.summary: In a second war Trajan subdued the whole country; Decebalus killed himself, and Dacia became a Roman province whose colonists made Latin its language, the root of Romanian.

events.title: Trajan's Column commemorates the conquest
events.summary: The Dacian wars were commemorated in Rome on Trajan's Column, a 38-metre marble monument whose spiral frieze of more than 2,600 figures narrates the two campaigns against the Dacians.

events.title: Aurelian abandons Dacia
events.summary: Under mounting frontier pressure the emperor Aurelian withdrew the Roman army and administration from Dacia, yet a Latin-speaking population persisted, carrying forward the Daco-Roman heritage.

figures.name: Decebalus
figures.blurb: The Dacian king who united his people and resisted Rome through two wars with Trajan. Defeated in 106 CE, he took his own life rather than be captured, and his kingdom became a Roman province.

figures.name: Trajan
figures.blurb: The emperor who conquered Dacia in the wars of 101–106 CE, annexing it as a province and settling colonists whose Latin speech became the foundation of the Romanian language.

pullquote.text: Trajan created a new province of Dacia north of the Danube, within the curve of the Carpathian Mountains; its Latin-speaking colonists left the language that became Romanian.
pullquote.attribution: On the Roman conquest of Dacia

eras.title: Wallachia, Moldavia and the Ottoman centuries
eras.standfirst: Two Romanian principalities arose on either side of the Carpathians, produced warrior-princes who defied empires, then endured long Ottoman suzerainty and the Phanariot regime.
body: In the early fourteenth century the Romanian-speaking lands south and east of the Carpathians coalesced into two principalities. In Wallachia the voivode Basarab I threw off Hungarian overlordship, winning recognition of his realm's autonomy after his victory over King Charles I of Hungary at Posada in 1330. To the east, the Vlach voivode Bogdan I crossed the Carpathians from Maramures around 1359 and wrested Moldavia from Hungarian vassalage, founding a second principality. These were the two states whose union would, five centuries later, form Romania.

Both principalities produced rulers celebrated for their defiance of greater powers. In Wallachia, Vlad III, called the Impaler (Vlad Tepes), waged a brutal resistance to the Ottomans in the 1450s and 1460s, his fearsome reputation later feeding the legend of Dracula. In Moldavia, Stephen the Great (Stefan cel Mare), prince from 1457 to 1504, won fame across Christian Europe for his long resistance to the Turks, crushing a far larger Ottoman army at Vaslui in 1475 and endowing a string of monasteries; the painted churches of northern Moldavia, their outer walls covered in Byzantine-inspired frescoes, are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Resistance could not, in the end, prevent submission. Over the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries Wallachia and Moldavia became tributary states under Ottoman suzerainty, retaining their own princes and institutions but bound to the Sublime Porte. From 1711 the Ottomans appointed their princes from among the Phanariots, wealthy Greek families of Constantinople, who governed both lands until 1821. The Phanariot period, marked by short tenures, heavy tribute and crushing taxation, became a byword for misrule, ending only with the upheavals of 1821.

Across the Carpathians, Transylvania followed a separate path under the Hungarian crown and later the Habsburgs, home to a mixed population of Romanians, Hungarians and German-speaking 'Saxon' settlers. The Saxons built fortified towns such as Sighisoara, whose well-preserved medieval citadel — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — testifies to a Transylvanian Saxon culture established from the twelfth century, even as the Romanian-speaking majority of the principalities remained outside this German and Magyar urban world.

events.title: Basarab I founds Wallachia
events.summary: The voivode Basarab I defeated King Charles I of Hungary at Posada, winning recognition of Wallachia's autonomy and establishing the first Romanian principality.

events.title: Bogdan I founds Moldavia
events.summary: The Vlach voivode Bogdan I crossed the Carpathians from Maramures and wrenched Moldavia from Hungarian vassalage, founding the second Romanian principality.

events.title: Stephen the Great's victory at Vaslui
events.summary: Stephen the Great of Moldavia crushed a much larger Ottoman army at Vaslui, one of his celebrated victories in a long resistance to the Turks that won him renown across Christian Europe.

events.title: Vlad III the Impaler resists the Ottomans
events.summary: Vlad III of Wallachia, remembered for impaling his enemies, fought a savage campaign against Ottoman power; his reputation later inspired the literary figure of Dracula.

events.title: The Phanariot regime
events.summary: Under Ottoman suzerainty the Porte appointed the princes of Wallachia and Moldavia from among the Greek Phanariot families of Constantinople, a period of heavy tribute and misrule that lasted until 1821.

events.title: Horezu Monastery and Brancovan art
events.summary: Prince Constantin Brancoveanu founded the monastery of Horezu in Wallachia, a masterpiece of the 'Brancovan' style whose school of mural and icon painting influenced religious art across the Balkans.

figures.name: Stephen the Great
figures.blurb: Prince of Moldavia from 1457 to 1504, Stephen won European renown for his resistance to the Ottoman Turks, defeating a great Ottoman army at Vaslui in 1475 and endowing the monasteries of northern Moldavia.

figures.name: Vlad III the Impaler
figures.blurb: A fifteenth-century prince of Wallachia whose ferocious resistance to the Ottomans and punishment of enemies by impalement earned him the name Vlad Tepes; his legend later shaped Bram Stoker's Dracula.

pullquote.text: Stephen the Great won renown in Europe for his long resistance to the Ottoman Turks.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the prince of Moldavia

eras.title: Union, independence and the Kingdom of Romania
eras.standfirst: The two principalities chose a single prince, won recognition of full independence at the Congress of Berlin and became a European kingdom.
body: The decisive step toward a Romanian state came in 1859. On 5 January the elective assembly of Moldavia chose Alexandru Ioan Cuza as its prince, and on 24 January the assembly of Wallachia elected the same man, uniting the two principalities in a single ruler. Cuza launched an ambitious modernisation: he reformed the justice and tax systems, carried through an agrarian reform that distributed land to some 400,000 peasant families, introduced the metric system and adopted a civil code modelled on the French. The united state increasingly called itself Romania, and in 1862 its assemblies and government merged in Bucharest.

Cuza's reforms made enemies, and in 1866 he was forced to abdicate. The throne passed to a German prince of the Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen house, who reigned as Carol I. During the Russo-Turkish War of 1877–78 Romania declared its independence and fought alongside Russia south of the Danube. The independence of the country was recognised by the great powers at the Congress of Berlin in July 1878, though Romania was obliged to cede southern Bessarabia to Russia and received the Dobruja in compensation.

Recognition as a sovereign state was crowned by elevation to a kingdom: in 1881 Romania was proclaimed a kingdom and Carol I became its first king. Under his long reign the country built railways, modernised its army and economy and took its place among the lesser powers of Europe, even as the position of its large peasant majority and the question of the millions of Romanians still living under Austro-Hungarian rule in Transylvania remained unresolved.

events.title: Union of the Principalities
events.summary: Moldavia (5 January) and Wallachia (24 January) elected Alexandru Ioan Cuza as their common prince, uniting the two principalities and founding the modern Romanian state.

events.title: Cuza's reforms
events.summary: Prince Cuza carried through agrarian reform giving land to some 400,000 peasant families, reformed law and taxation, introduced the metric system and a French-modelled civil code.

events.title: Independence recognised at the Congress of Berlin
events.summary: After fighting in the Russo-Turkish War, Romania's full independence was recognised by the great powers at the Congress of Berlin in 1878, though it ceded southern Bessarabia to Russia.

events.title: Kingdom of Romania proclaimed
events.summary: Romania was proclaimed a kingdom and Carol I, prince since 1866, became its first king, completing the country's emergence as a sovereign European state.

figures.name: Alexandru Ioan Cuza
figures.blurb: Elected prince of both Moldavia and Wallachia in 1859, Cuza united the principalities and drove a programme of reform — land to the peasants, a modern civil code and the metric system — before being forced to abdicate in 1866.

figures.name: Carol I
figures.blurb: Prince from 1866 and first king from 1881, the Hohenzollern Carol I led Romania to independence in the war of 1877–78, secured its recognition at the Congress of Berlin and modernised the new kingdom over a long reign.

pullquote.text: The Union has been accomplished. The Romanian nation has been founded.
pullquote.attribution: Alexandru Ioan Cuza, on the union of 1859

eras.title: Greater Romania: the Great War and the union of Transylvania
eras.standfirst: The First World War doubled Romania's size, uniting Transylvania, Bessarabia and Bukovina in 'Greater Romania' and confirming the transfer of Transylvania from Hungary at Trianon.
body: Romania entered the First World War in 1916 on the side of the Allies, hoping to win the Romanian-inhabited lands of Austria-Hungary, above all Transylvania. The war went badly at first — much of the country was occupied — but the collapse of the Russian, Austro-Hungarian and Ottoman empires by 1918 transformed Romania's prospects. As the old order dissolved, the Romanian-majority provinces moved to join the kingdom.

The defining moment came on 1 December 1918, when a great national assembly of Romanians met at Alba Iulia in Transylvania and proclaimed the union of Transylvania with the Kingdom of Romania. The assembly drew delegates from across the Romanian-inhabited lands and was attended by a vast crowd. Together with the union of Bessarabia and Bukovina the same year, it created 'Greater Romania' (Romania Mare), nearly doubling the country's territory and population and bringing large Hungarian and German minorities within its borders.

These gains were enshrined in the post-war settlement. The Treaty of Trianon, signed on 4 June 1920, formally transferred Transylvania and part of the Banat from Hungary to Romania. The treaty shore Hungary of roughly two-thirds of its pre-war territory and population, leaving a lasting grievance in Budapest and a Hungarian minority in Romania, and it made the union of Transylvania the central, contested fact of twentieth-century Romanian–Hungarian relations. The interwar Kingdom of Romania was the largest the country has ever been.

events.title: Romania enters the First World War
events.summary: Romania joined the Allies in 1916 seeking the Romanian lands of Austria-Hungary, especially Transylvania; despite early defeats, the collapse of the empires opened the way to union.

events.title: Union of Transylvania with Romania
events.summary: A great national assembly of Romanians at Alba Iulia proclaimed the union of Transylvania with the Kingdom of Romania, the central act in the creation of Greater Romania.

events.title: Greater Romania
events.summary: With Bessarabia, Bukovina and Transylvania joined to the kingdom, Romania nearly doubled in size, gaining large Hungarian and German minorities within its enlarged borders.

events.title: Treaty of Trianon
events.summary: The Treaty of Trianon formally transferred Transylvania and part of the Banat from Hungary to Romania, stripping Hungary of about two-thirds of its territory and leaving a lasting dispute.

figures.name: Ferdinand I
figures.blurb: King during the First World War, Ferdinand I led Romania into the Allied camp and presided over the union of Transylvania, Bessarabia and Bukovina, becoming the monarch of 'Greater Romania'.

figures.name: Queen Marie of Romania
figures.blurb: Wife of Ferdinand I, Marie became a celebrated symbol of Romania during the First World War and lobbied the Allied powers at the Paris peace talks for recognition of Greater Romania's enlarged borders.

pullquote.text: The union of Transylvania with Romania, proclaimed at Alba Iulia on 1 December 1918, was confirmed by the Treaty of Trianon in 1920.
pullquote.attribution: On the making of Greater Romania

eras.title: Dismemberment, the Second World War and communist rule
eras.standfirst: In 1940 Romania lost a third of its land to its neighbours; war, the Axis and a Soviet-backed takeover followed, ending in the dictatorship of Nicolae Ceausescu.
body: The fragile order of Greater Romania collapsed in the summer of 1940. On 28 June 1940 the Soviet Union seized Bessarabia and northern Bukovina; on 30 August, under German and Italian arbitration, the Second Vienna Award forced Romania to cede northern Transylvania to Hungary; and in September southern Dobruja was ceded to Bulgaria. In all Romania lost about thirty per cent of its territory and population. The catastrophe brought down King Carol II, and General Ion Antonescu took power, leading Romania into the Axis on 20 November 1940 and into the war against the Soviet Union, during which the regime carried out the deportation and murder of large numbers of Jews and Roma.

As the tide of war turned, the young King Michael I led a coup against Antonescu on 23 August 1944, switching Romania to the Allied side. The peace settlement at the end of the war was mixed: the Paris peace treaty restored northern Transylvania to Romania, but Bessarabia and northern Bukovina remained in the Soviet Union and southern Dobruja in Bulgaria. Bessarabia, with its overwhelmingly Romanian-speaking population, was absorbed into the USSR as the core of the future republic of Moldova, leaving the bond between Romania and Moldova one of shared language and identity rather than a single state.

Liberated by the Red Army, Romania was drawn into the Soviet sphere; a communist regime was installed, the monarchy abolished in 1947, and a People's Republic proclaimed. From 1965 the country was ruled by Nicolae Ceausescu, whose increasingly autocratic regime combined a foreign policy independent of Moscow with a stifling cult of personality, secret-police surveillance and, in the 1980s, severe austerity to repay foreign debt. By the late 1980s Romania was the most rigid and repressive of the Warsaw Pact states.

events.title: Soviet seizure of Bessarabia and northern Bukovina
events.summary: The Soviet Union demanded and took Bessarabia and northern Bukovina from Romania, the first of the 1940 territorial losses; Bessarabia, largely Romanian-speaking, became the core of the later Republic of Moldova.

events.title: Second Vienna Award and Southern Dobruja
events.summary: Under German and Italian arbitration Romania ceded northern Transylvania to Hungary; in September southern Dobruja was ceded to Bulgaria, costing Romania about 30% of its territory and people.

events.title: Romania joins the Axis
events.summary: After the loss of King Carol II's throne, General Ion Antonescu took power and brought Romania into the Axis, later joining the war against the Soviet Union and presiding over the murder of hundreds of thousands of Jews and Roma.

events.title: Communist takeover and the People's Republic
events.summary: After the war Romania was drawn into the Soviet bloc; the Paris treaty restored northern Transylvania, but Bessarabia stayed Soviet, the monarchy was abolished and a communist People's Republic proclaimed.

events.title: The Ceausescu dictatorship
events.summary: Nicolae Ceausescu ruled Romania from 1965, pursuing a foreign policy independent of Moscow while imposing a personality cult, pervasive secret-police control and harsh 1980s austerity.

figures.name: Michael I
figures.blurb: Romania's last king, Michael I led the August 1944 coup that overthrew Antonescu and brought Romania over to the Allies. He was forced to abdicate by the communists in 1947.

figures.name: Nicolae Ceausescu
figures.blurb: Leader of communist Romania from 1965, Ceausescu built an autocratic regime around a cult of personality and harsh austerity. He and his wife Elena were overthrown and executed in the revolution of December 1989.

pullquote.text: During the summer and autumn of 1940 Romania lost about 30 percent of its territory and population.

eras.title: Revolution, democracy and the European turn
eras.standfirst: A violent revolution ended the Ceausescu dictatorship, and a democratic Romania joined NATO and the European Union, settling its Black Sea boundary with Ukraine at the world court.
body: The end of communist Romania came suddenly and violently. In mid-December 1989 protests erupted in Timisoara, sparked by the regime's attempt to remove a dissident pastor, and spread to Bucharest. On 22 December, as the army went over to the demonstrators, Nicolae and Elena Ceausescu fled the capital by helicopter; captured within days, they were tried by a hastily convened military court and executed on 25 December 1989. More than a thousand people died in the upheaval, the only one of the 1989 revolutions in the Soviet bloc to end in such bloodshed and in the execution of its leader.

The new Romania rebuilt itself as a democracy and a market economy and turned decisively toward the West. It joined the North Atlantic Treaty Organization on 29 March 2004, when it and six other states deposited their instruments of accession, and it became a member of the European Union on 1 January 2007, alongside Bulgaria, raising the EU to twenty-seven members. The bond with the Republic of Moldova — independent since 1991 but sharing the Romanian language and a common history through Bessarabia — remained close, while the wartime losses to the east were not reversed.

One legacy of the redrawn borders was settled in The Hague. Romania and Ukraine disputed the maritime boundary in the north-western Black Sea, including the weight to be given to Ukraine's tiny Serpents' (Snake) Island in dividing the continental shelf and exclusive economic zones. In its judgment of 3 February 2009 in Maritime Delimitation in the Black Sea (Romania v. Ukraine), the International Court of Justice fixed a single maritime boundary and ruled that Serpents' Island should have no effect on the delimitation beyond the twelve-nautical-mile arc of its own territorial sea — a binding decision that resolved the dispute and clarified the law on small islands.

events.title: The Romanian Revolution
events.summary: Protests beginning in Timisoara spread to Bucharest; the army turned against the regime, the Ceausescus fled on 22 December and were executed on 25 December 1989, ending communist rule.

events.title: Romania joins NATO
events.summary: Romania formally joined NATO on 29 March 2004, depositing its instrument of accession in Washington alongside six other new members in the alliance's largest round of enlargement.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: On 1 January 2007 Romania, with Bulgaria, joined the European Union, raising it to twenty-seven member states and completing Romania's integration into the West.

events.title: Moldova's independence and the Romanian bond
events.summary: As the Soviet Union dissolved, the former Bessarabia declared independence as the Republic of Moldova, immediately recognised by Romania, with which it shares the Romanian language and a common history.

events.title: ICJ Black Sea boundary with Ukraine
events.summary: The International Court of Justice fixed the Romania–Ukraine maritime boundary in the Black Sea, ruling that Ukraine's Serpents' Island would have no effect beyond its 12-mile territorial sea.

figures.name: Ion Iliescu
figures.blurb: A former communist official who led the National Salvation Front that took power in December 1989, Iliescu became Romania's first post-communist president and a dominant figure of the 1990s transition.

figures.name: Klaus Iohannis
figures.blurb: An ethnic German from Transylvania and former mayor of Sibiu, Iohannis served as president from 2014, embodying Romania's anchoring in the European Union and NATO and its multi-ethnic Transylvanian heritage.

pullquote.text: The Court found that Serpents' Island should have no effect on the delimitation other than that stemming from the 12-nautical-mile arc of its territorial sea.
pullquote.attribution: International Court of Justice, Maritime Delimitation in the Black Sea (2009)

figures.name: Decebalus
figures.blurb: King of the Dacians who resisted Rome through two wars with Trajan; his defeat in 106 CE turned his kingdom into the Roman province whose Latin speech became Romanian.

figures.name: Stephen the Great
figures.blurb: The great prince of Moldavia, renowned across Europe for his resistance to the Ottoman Turks and his victory at Vaslui in 1475, and for the painted churches he endowed.

figures.name: Alexandru Ioan Cuza
figures.blurb: Elected prince of both Moldavia and Wallachia in 1859, Cuza united the principalities into the modern Romanian state and drove its first great wave of reform.

figures.name: Carol I
figures.blurb: Prince from 1866 and first king from 1881, Carol I led Romania to independence, recognised at the Congress of Berlin in 1878, and modernised the new kingdom.
